content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
November 23,
1992
John Glenn
36601 Cuenca Court
Fremont,
California
94536
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
1—92—225
Dear Mr.
Glenn:
You have requested advice concerning application of the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
“Act”)l to your duties as a member of the Board of Directors of
the San Francisco Bay Area Rapid Transit District
(“BART”).
Since your letter does not seek advice regarding a specific
pending governmental decision,
we treat your letter as a request
for informal assistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation
18329.2
QUESTION
Under the Act,
may you participate in decisions regarding the
various construction contracts for the extension of the
DART
system in San Mateo County?
CONCLUSION
You may participate in the decisions regarding the various
construction contracts for the
BART extension only if there will
not be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory refer
ences are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Com
mission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et .
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—92-225
Page
2
either your business,
John Glenn Adjusters,
or upon the San Mateo
County Transit District
(“SamTrans”).
FACTS
You are the sole owner of John Glenn Adjusters and
Administrators
(“Adjusters”),
an insurance adjusting business.
During the past twelve months your business has received more than
$250 in income for insurance adjusting services under contract
with SaxnTrans.
SamTrans has been a client for approximately ten
years.
BART and SamTrans have entered into a “Comprehensive Agree
ment Pertaining to
BART System Extensions.”
This agreement
provides for an extension of
BART to the San Francisco
International Airport in San Mateo County.
BART is preparing
plans and specifications for contracts to extend the system.
Most
of these contracts will be awarded pursuant to competitive bid
ding.
Pursuant to the comprehensive agreement,
BART will pay 75% of
the costs from a grant of federal funds and SamTrans will pay a
local share of
25%.
SainTrans will also pay a capital contribution
to
BART, with a series of installments as set forth in the agree
ment.
Although SamTrans will provide part of the funding for the
project,
the contracts will be between
BART and the individual
contractors;
SamTrans will not be a party to the contracts.
ANALYSIS
The Act prohibits a public official from making, participat
ing in,
or using her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which she knows or has reason to know she has a
financial interest.
(Section 87100.)
A public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of her immediate family or on,
among other
things:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to


Our File No.
1—92—225
Page
3
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty dol
lars
($250)
or more in value provided to, received
by or promised to the public official within
12
months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
(e)
Any donor of,
or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to, received by,
or promised to the public
official within
12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.
Section 87103(a)—(e).
As a public official,
you may not participate in a decision
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally,
on your business or on any source of income of
$250 or more within the
12 months prior to the time when the deci
sion is made.
(Section 87103(a),
(C)
and (d).)
Foreseeability
The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that they will occur.
To be foresee
able, the effects of a decision must be more than a mere possibil
ity; however, certainty is not required.
(Downey Cares v.
Downey
Community Development Corn.
(1987)
196 Cal.App.3d 983,
989—991;
Witt v.
Morrow
(1977)
70 Cal.App.3d 817,
822;
In re Thorner
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
198.)
The Act seeks to prevent more than actual
conflicts of interest, it seeks to prevent even the appearance of
a possible conflict of interest.
(Witt v.
Morrow,
supra. at 823.)
Materiality
Since Adjusters derives income of over $250
a year from
SamTrans,
SamTrans is considered a source of income to you under
the Act.
(Section 87103(c).)
If a decision regarding the
construction contracts will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally,
on either your business or on a source of income to
you,
you must disqualify yourself from participating in the deci
sion.


Our File No.
1-92-225
Page
4
Therefore,
you must determine if it is reasonably foreseeable
that Adjusters will be materially affected by any of the construc
tion contracts decisions.
Please consult the following factors in
Regulation 18702.2(g)
3
to make this determination:
(1)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or
(2)
The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more;
or
(3)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.
In the analysis,
you also have to determine if there will be
a material financial effect on SamTrans,
a non—profit public
entity,
which is also a source of income to you.
Regulation
18702.5
(copy enclosed) provides the guidelines for determining if
the financial effect is material when a nonprofit entity,
which is
a source of income,
is indirectly involved in the decision.
You
stated that the construction contracts will be between BART and
the individual contractors;
SamTrans will not be a party to the
contract.
However, under the comprehensive agreement,
SamTrans
will pay
25% of the contract price.
Thus,
SamTrans will be
financially affected by the decision.
The question is, will the
effect be material.
After review of the appropriate section of the regulations,
you may participate in the decisions regarding the various
construction contracts only if you determine that there will not
be a material financial effect on either Adjusters or SamTrans.
This assumes that the financial size of your company is such
that it is covered by subdivision
(g)
of 18702.2.
If it is not,
please review the regulation to apply the appropriate standard.


Our File No.
1-92-225
Page
5
I trust that this letter has answered your questions.
If you
have any further questions regarding this matter,
please contact
me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
By:
JillJR. techer
Counsel,
Legal Division
SH:JS:dl
Enclosure
